retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator's sensory innervation comes from the long buccal nerve, a branch of the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V2). Motor innervation of the muscle is via the temporal and cervical divisions of the facial nerve (CN VII). [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Facial Colliculus -- Muscles -- Buccinator
retrieved_docs.content: The buccal branch of CN VII innervates the muscle. It is responsible for pulling the cheek against teeth and puckering.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lateral Pterygoid Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral pterygoid muscle receives innervation from the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve. The main trunk of the mandibular nerve divides into the anterior and the posterior division. The anterior division gives off three motor branches. One of the motor branches is the nerve to the lateral pterygoid, a paired nerve (one for each belly) innervating the lateral pterygoid muscle. According to some authors, this muscle may also receive innervation from the anterior, middle, and posterior deep temporal nerves, the masseteric nerve, and sometimes from the buccal nerve. They innervate different muscle parts, resulting in selective activation, which ensures coordination of movements and reduces stress on the temporomandibular joint. There is a general acceptance that the lateral pterygoid muscle is innervated by the lateral pterygoid nerve, which arises from the mandibular nerve. [3] Although both bellies receive their innervation from the mandibular nerve, different nerves may innervate each of them. [4] [10] Kim et al described a typical nerve distribution pattern where the superior belly is innervated by the buccal nerve and the inferior belly by the mandibular nerve trunk. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Levator Anguli Oris Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The facial nerve provides motor control to the facial expression muscles, posterior digastric muscle belly, stylohyoid, and stapedius. Upon emerging from the temporal bone, the facial nerve travels through the parotid gland, separating the deep and superficial lobes, and continues distally beneath the face's superficial musculoaponeurotic system. The mimetic muscles are contiguous with this musculoaponeurotic layer. Thus, facial expression muscles, except the buccinator, mentalis, and LAO, are innervated from their deep surfaces. The buccinator, mentalis, and LAO lie deeper within the face and are innervated from their superficial surfaces by the facial nerve's terminal buccal branch. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccal Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The buccal nerve runs downwards and forwards from the temporobuccal or temporobuccinator nerve to arrive at the external face of the buccinator muscle hidden by the fat pad of Bichat. Discussion on its relation to the tendon of insertion of the temporal muscle has led to the conclusion that it does not go through the tendon, but is situated firstly medial and then anterior to the tendon. [2] The buccal nerve divides into superficial and deep branches. The superficial or cutaneous branches of the buccal nerve end in the deep layer of the skin of the cheek and anastomose with branches of the facial nerve. The deep or mucosal branches pierce the buccinator muscle and distribute throughout the buccal mucosa. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The fibers of all 3 bundles run anteriorly and descend through the modiolus, thus forming the musculature of the cheek. The buccinator fibers blend with orbicularis oris muscle fibers. The maxillary bundle fibers blend with the orbicularis oris muscle fibers at the mandibular lip. The mandibular bundle fibers run superiorly to the maxillary lip. The longitudinal bundle fibers travel toward the oral commissures to insert into the upper and lower lips. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Corticobulbar Tract -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Corticobulbar tract carries upper motor neuron input to motor nuclei of trigeminal, facial, glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory, and hypoglossal nerves. The motor component of trigeminal nerves supplies muscles of mastication. The facial nerve supplies the muscles of facial expression. Glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory nerve innervates muscles of the pharynx, muscles of larynx, sternocleidomastoid, and trapezius muscle. Tongue muscles receive fibers from the hypoglossal nerve. The corticobulbar tract indirectly affects muscles supplied by third, fourth, and sixth cranial nerve through medial longitudinal fasciculus and paramedian part of the pontine formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Facial nerve damage can lead to buccinator paralysis and difficult mastication. Loss of buccinator function can cause repeated cheek mucosal lacerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator, orbicularis oris, and pharyngeal constrictors form a functional unit—the buccinator mechanism—essential to orofacial functions like swallowing, sucking, whistling, chewing, and vowel pronunciation. Food accumulates in the vestibule during chewing if the buccinator is weakened or paralyzed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator muscle is a square, bilateral muscle situated in the mobile and adaptable cheek area. [1] Couper and Myot coined the term "buccinator" in 1694, relating to blowing a trumpet. Notably, this muscle puffs out the cheeks, thus it is also referred to as the "trumpeter's muscle." [2] The buccinator is one of the first muscles to be activated by sucking during infancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The SMAS is contiguous anteriorly with the facial muscles, inferiorly with the platysma, and superiorly with the temporoparietal fascia and frontalis muscle. CN VII branches lie deep to the facial muscles in these areas except the buccinator, mentalis, and levator anguli oris, which are innervated on their superficial surfaces because of their deep location. [18] The upper facial muscles frontalis, corrugator supercilii, and procerus are innervated by the frontal branch (also known as the temporal branch). [19] The orbicularis oculi receives innervation from CN VII's frontal and zygomatic branches. The zygomatic and buccal branches control the midfacial muscles. [20] CN VII's marginal mandibular branch supplies the depressor labii inferioris, depressor anguli oris, and mentalis. The cervical branch supplies the platysma muscle. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator muscle fibers originate from 3 areas, giving rise to 3 muscular bundles. Two of the muscular bundles have a bony origin. The maxillary bundle arises from the buccal portion of the maxilla's alveolar process. The mandibular bundle originates from the buccal portion of the mandible's alveolar process. The longitudinal bundle emerges from the pterygomandibular raphe. The raphe connects the buccinator's posterior fibers to the superior constrictor muscle, the most superior pharyngeal constrictor (see Image . Muscles of the Cheek and Pharynx).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Deglutition occurs in phases. The buccinator plays a role in the oral and pharyngeal phases of swallowing. The buccinator and orbicularis oris generate a peristaltic wave-like contraction during the oral phase. This wave is subsequently transmitted to the pharynx. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Risorius Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The risorius muscle is innervated, like all muscles of facial expression, by the facial nerve, to which it is directly connected. There is debate over which branch of the facial nerve actually innervates the risorius, but most agree that it is either one of the buccal branches or the marginal mandibular branch, and it may receive motor innervation from both. The buccal branches of the facial nerve arise from the facial nerve deep to the parotid gland and just posterior to the superficial and deep masseter muscles upon which it sits and has branches that flank the risorius muscle on its superior and inferior aspects. The marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve is a tributary from the facial nerve just distal to the most distal buccal branch, and courses down the angle of the jaw and along the posterior aspect of the mandible. Sensory innervation of the cheek come from the buccal nerve, a branch of the anterior division of the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve via general somatic afferent fibers.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Cheeks -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The cheek region receives innervation from the facial nerve and the trigeminal nerve. The facial nerve will ascend toward the face after exiting the stylomastoid foramen. The facial nerve will travel through the parotid gland. The facial nerve will split the parotid gland into two main lobes. As the facial nerve exits the parotid gland, it splits into five main branches: frontal, zygomatic, buccal, marginal mandibular, and cervical. The buccal branch will innervate the majority of the muscles in the cheek region except for the orbicularis oculi and the masseter muscle. Innervation of the orbicularis oculi muscle is from the zygomatic branch of the facial nerve. The facial nerve's innervation will coordinate the contraction of facial muscles. The different combinations of facial muscle contractions will produce various facial expressions. The trigeminal nerve will give sensory innervation to the entire face, including the cheeks. The trigeminal nerve also delivers motor innervation to the masseter muscle. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Eye Corrugator Muscle -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The corrugator supercilii muscles have a dual nerve supply from branches of the temporal, zygomatic, and buccal branches of the facial nerve. The motor nerve supply of the corrugator supercilii muscles originates from the frontal branch of the temporal division of the facial nerve while the zygomatic branch appears to innervate the oblique head. After receiving innervation from the zygomatic branch, the buccal branch forms the angular nerve to supply the corrugator supercilii muscles and procerus. [6]

retrieved_docs.content: bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) when it involves nerve findings, and muscular atrophy/Bell’s palsy (وهن عضلي وبيل), which reflect the nerves’ integral role in motor and sensory function. Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses a diverse group of conditions, including اعتلال الأعصاب السكري (diabetic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث (multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease), التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb), داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية (horse‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder), and وهن عضلي وبيل (myasthenic muscle weakness). By classifying these entities under Other disorders of nervous system, clinicians recognize that each condition involves neurological pathology—whether peripheral nerve damage, hereditary motor‑sensory neuropathy, autonomic dysfunction, or neuromuscular junction impairment—while also acknowledging their distinct etiologies and clinical presentations. This unified classification aids in systematic diagnosis, facilitates appropriate referral to neurology specialists, and supports coordinated management strategies tailored to the specific neurological features of each disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of distinct neurological conditions. It is classified as تكزز, as اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), as اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني (multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease), as التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), as التهاب العصب الأحادي (mononeuritis), as الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic atrophy of the lower limb), as داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and as وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and Bell’s palsy). By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system, clinicians can recognize that they share a common classification despite differing pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and management strategies, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 108
retrieved_docs.content: عضلي), Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), Bell’s palsy (شلل بل), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), horseshoe‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), Willis‑muscle weakness (وهن عضلي وبيل), lower‑limb reflexive sclerosis (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي), single‑nerve neuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) and multi‑single‑nerve neuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد). Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) are the primary anatomic locus for the majority of these conditions, including Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease, muscular neuropathy, all forms of polyneuropathy, horseshoe‑tail syndrome, trigeminal neuralgia pain and Bell’s palsy. Damage to peripheral nerves of the thorax represents a specific manifestation of peripheral neuropathy, and Neurologic qualifiers extend to alcoholic polyneuropathy, trigeminal neuralgia pain and Bell’s palsy. Common clinical findings across

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) is connected to paralysis (شلل), indicating that disorders affecting both nerves and muscles can result in loss of motor function. Muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات) also has a relationship with paralysis, reinforcing the link between impaired muscle function and the development of paralysis. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are related to muscle disorder, suggesting that pathological changes in joint‑related structures can influence muscle health and thereby contribute to paralytic outcomes. More specific conditions, such as lesion in the sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are identified as narrower relationships to paralysis, highlighting that focal nerve injuries or dysfunctions are precise causes of paralytic deficits. Together, these concepts illustrate how a spectrum of neuromuscular, muscular, and peripheral nerve pathologies are interrelated and can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of evaluating both muscle and nerve integrity in patients presenting with motor impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال عصبي عضلي (muscle neuropathy) is linked to شلل (paralysis), indicating that neuropathic involvement of muscle tissue can result in loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are associated with اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), showing that pathological changes in peri‑articular structures often affect muscle integrity. اضطراب العضلات itself has a relationship with شلل, reinforcing the concept that muscle disorders can progress to or coexist with paralysis. اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) includes ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (upper facial atrophy) as a specific manifestation, and it also has a narrower relationship with شلل, suggesting that facial nerve pathology may contribute to localized or generalized motor weakness. آفة في العصب الوركي (lesion in the sciatic nerve) similarly has a narrower relationship with شلل, highlighting that focal peripheral nerve lesions can directly produce paralysis of the innervated limb. Collectively, these connections illustrate how neuromuscular and musculoskeletal disorders—ranging from muscle neuropathy and tendon‑synovial disease to facial and sciatic nerve lesions—are interrelated pathways that can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of both nerve and muscle pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: of the tendon sheath (التهاب زَلِيْلِ الوتَر) is mapped from carpal tunnel syndrome, and unspecified single‑nerve inflammation in the upper limbs is related to tarsal tunnel syndrome, reflecting overlapping mechanisms. Disease‑has‑primary‑anatomic‑site links peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) to Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease and injury of peripheral thoracic nerves to peripheral neuropathy. Muscular disorders (اضطراب العضلات) and pathologies of the synovium, tendon, and bursa contribute to paralysis, underscoring the interaction between musculoskeletal and neurologic systems. Collectively, these entities delineate a network in which vascular, inflammatory, toxic, genetic, metabolic, traumatic, and compressive etiologies converge on peripheral nerves, frequently resulting in motor or autonomic impairment, and where peripheral nerve decompression constitutes a central therapeutic approach.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: palsy, combined myasthenic‑Bell’s palsy, and sciatic nerve lesion, all associated with motor weakness or paralysis. Muscular and neuromuscular disorders (muscle disorder, muscular neuropathy, broader neuromuscular disease) are also classified under this category, linking muscle pathology to peripheral nerve involvement and secondary paralysis. Ancillary entries such as acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) and disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are connected to muscle disorder and secondary paralysis, highlighting the overlap between neuropathic and adjacent musculoskeletal inflammation. Overall, the taxonomy connects genetic, metabolic, toxic, inflammatory, traumatic, and compressive insults to peripheral and cranial nerves, with clinical outcomes of pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: النفق الكعبري), **phantom‑limb syndrome**, **horseshoe kidney with neurogenic bladder** (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), and **reflex sympathetic dystrophy of the lower limb** (الحثل الانعكاسي الودي للطرف السفلي). Procedural interventions include **decompression of peripheral nerves**, **carpal tunnel release** (تخفيف ضغط النفق الرسغي), and **incision of nerve** (part of the decompression peripheral‑nerve family). **Cranial‑nerve disorders** are represented by **olfactory nerve disorder** (اضطراب العصب الشمي), **facial nerve disorder** (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), and the unspecified eye‑and‑adnexal disease **“Aandoening van oog en adnexen, niet gespecificeerd.”** **Motor and muscle disorders** include **muscle disorder** (اضطراب العضلات), **neuromuscular disease** (اعتلال عصبي عضلي), **muscular atrophy** (وهن عضلي وبيل), and the broader label **“System disorder of the nervous system.”** Rare hereditary syndromes such as **Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease** (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث) are linked to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) serve as the primary anatomic site for several distinct neurological disorders, including Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), the horse‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), and Beals‑type muscular dystrophy (وهن عضلي وبيل). In addition to being the main locus of these diseases, peripheral nerves are frequently identified as the site of clinical findings in a range of neuropathic conditions. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري) and idiopathic progressive neuropathy of unknown cause (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب) both manifest as damage to peripheral nerve fibers, producing sensory and motor deficits. Inflammatory processes such as multifocal mononeuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد) also target peripheral nerves, leading to focal nerve pain and weakness. Although acute infectious bronchiolitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي) primarily involves the respiratory tract, occasional reports describe associated peripheral nerve irritation or secondary neuropathic symptoms, suggesting that systemic infection can extend its impact to the peripheral nervous system. Collectively, these relationships underscore the central role of peripheral nerves in both primary neurogenetic diseases and secondary neuropathic manifestations, highlighting the importance of thorough neurological assessment and targeted management in patients presenting with peripheral nerve involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base groups a wide array of neurologic and neuromuscular conditions under the umbrella of **Other disorders of the nervous system**, emphasizing their inter‑relationships with pain, paralysis, and sensory disturbance. Generic pain classifications (including “Pain, not elsewhere classified”) and specific pain entities such as **trigeminal nerve pain** and **facial nerve disorders** (including **facial paralysis** and upper‑facial atrophy) are linked to peripheral nerve pathology. Lesions of individual peripheral nerves—**plantar nerve**, **calcaneal (tibial) nerve**, **sciatic nerve**, **thoracic peripheral nerves**, and hemorrhage into a nerve trunk—are each associated with localized motor loss, foot deformities, or broader paralytic outcomes. **Peripheral neuropathy** is a central subcategory connected to pain and paralysis and subdivided into numerous etiologies: **alcoholic polyneuropathy**, **diabetic neuropathy**, **idiopathic progressive neuropathy**,
